root_text.sn56.13:1.3: Dukkhaṁ ariyasaccaṁ, dukkhasamudayaṁ ariyasaccaṁ, dukkhanirodhaṁ ariyasaccaṁ dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā ariyasaccaṁ.
root_text.sn56.13:3.2: Yāyaṁ taṇhā ponobbhavikā nandirāgasahagatā tatratatrābhinandinī, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn56.13:4.2: Yo tassāyeva taṇhāya asesavirāganirodho cāgo paṭinissaggo mutti anālayo—
root_text.sn56.13:5.4: idaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā ariyasaccaṁ.
root_text.sn56.13:6.1: Tasmātiha, bhikkhave, ‘idaṁ dukkhan’ti yogo karaṇīyo …pe… ‘ayaṁ dukkhanirodhagāminī paṭipadā’ti yogo karaṇīyo”ti.

translation_text.sn56.13:0.2: 2. Rolling Forth the Wheel of Dhamma
translation_text.sn56.13:1.1: “Mendicants, there are these four noble truths.
translation_text.sn56.13:1.3: The noble truths of suffering, the origin of suffering, the cessation of suffering, and the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering.
translation_text.sn56.13:2.1: And what is the noble truth of suffering?
translation_text.sn56.13:2.2: You should say: ‘The five grasping aggregates’.
translation_text.sn56.13:2.3: That is: form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn56.13:2.4: This is called the noble truth of suffering.
translation_text.sn56.13:3.1: And what is the noble truth of the origin of suffering?
translation_text.sn56.13:3.2: It’s the craving that leads to future lives, mixed up with relishing and greed, taking pleasure wherever it lands. That is,
translation_text.sn56.13:3.3: craving for sensual pleasures, craving for existence, and craving for nonexistence.
translation_text.sn56.13:3.4: This is called the noble truth of the origin of suffering.
translation_text.sn56.13:4.1: And what is the noble truth of the cessation of suffering?
translation_text.sn56.13:4.2: It’s the fading away and cessation of that very same craving with nothing left over; giving it away, letting it go, releasing it, and not clinging to it.
translation_text.sn56.13:4.3: This is called the noble truth of the cessation of suffering.
translation_text.sn56.13:5.1: And what is the noble truth of the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering?
translation_text.sn56.13:5.2: It is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn56.13:5.3: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn56.13:5.4: This is called the noble truth of the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering.
translation_text.sn56.13:5.5: These are the four noble truths.
translation_text.sn56.13:6.1: That’s why you should practice meditation …”

variant_text.sn56.13:1.3: dukkhasamudayaṁ ariyasaccaṁ, dukkhanirodhaṁ ariyasaccaṁ → dukkhasamudayo ariyasaccaṁ dukkhanirodho ariyasaccaṁ (bj, sya-all)
variant_text.sn56.13:2.2: seyyathidaṁ → katame pañca? seyyathīdaṁ (bj); katame pañca (sya-all, km); seyyathīdaṁ (pts1ed)
